notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Piping Fundamentals and Design
notes.content:
PIPING FUNDAMENTALS:

PURPOSE AND FUNCTION: Transports fluids and fluid/solid mixtures between equipment. Design must account for temperature, pressure, flow rate, chemical/physical properties, contamination, and shock loads.

DESIGN FACTORS:
• Temperature affects material properties, expansion, and thermal stress
• Pressure determines wall thickness, material selection, support design
• Flow rate/velocity affects erosion, corrosion, and pressure drop
• Shock loads (water hammer, steam hammer, thermal shock, seismic) require robust design
• Changes in direction require proper support and restraint

MATERIAL SELECTION:
• Low-carbon steel: economical, prone to rust/corrosion
• Low-alloy steel: high-temperature applications, improved wear/fatigue/corrosion resistance
• Stainless steel: corrosion resistant, minimizes corrosion product buildup
• Copper alloys: excellent corrosion resistance, good conductivity
• Nickel alloys: high temperature/pressure applications, high cost
• Plastic: corrosion resistant, lightweight, limited temperature range

PIPE SUPPORT SYSTEMS:
• Rigid restraints: fixed, prevent movement
• Spring hangers: support vertical gravity loading, allow thermal movement
• Snubbers: limit seismic motion while allowing thermal growth

THERMAL EFFECTS:
• Thermal loops/bends: accommodate thermal expansion
• Expansion joints: allow controlled linear expansion
• Insulation: controls heat loss/gain and protects personnel

notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Valve Functions and Basic Construction
notes.content:
FIVE PRINCIPAL VALVE FUNCTIONS:

1. STARTING/STOPPING FLOW: Isolation to completely stop or start fluid flow (gate valves, ball valves, plug valves)

2. THROTTLING FLOW: Control flow rate without stopping completely, requires throttling-capable design (globe valves, needle valves)

3. CONTROLLING DIRECTION OF FLOW: Routes fluid to different paths (directional control valves, check valves)

4. REGULATING PRESSURE: Maintains desired system pressure (pressure regulators, pressure-reducing valves)

5. RELIEVING PRESSURE: Protection against overpressure (relief valves, safety valves)

BASIC VALVE CONSTRUCTION:
• Body: contains internal flow path, seats, and internal parts
• Bonnet: upper section that seals the body
• Disk: moves to open/close or throttle flow
• Seat rings: provide seal surface for the disk
• Stem: connects disk to actuator/handwheel
• Backseat: allows packing adjustment without system depressurization
• Packing/stuffing box: compression seal prevents stem leakage

STEM DESIGNS:
• Rising stem: stem moves upward when valve opens (visual position indicator)
• Non-rising stem: stem does not move (position not visually obvious)

notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Pressure Types and Relationships
notes.content:
PRESSURE FUNDAMENTALS:

DEFINITION: Pressure = Force / Area (P = F/A). Units: psi, Pa, bar, atm. Normal atmospheric pressure = 14.7 psia = 101.3 kPa.

HYDROSTATIC PRESSURE: Pressure from weight of fluid column above reference point. Formula: P = rho x g x h. Increases with depth. Acts in all directions equally.

ABSOLUTE PRESSURE: Measured from perfect vacuum (zero reference). Units: psia. Thermodynamic calculations require absolute pressure. Example: 10 psig = 24.7 psia.

GAGE PRESSURE (PSIG): Pressure above atmospheric. Formula: Pgage = Pabsolute - Patm. Common pressure gauge indication.

VACUUM PRESSURE: Pressure below atmospheric. Formula: Pvac = Patm - Pabs. Used in condenser and pump suction applications.

DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE (PSID): Difference between two locations. Used for filter drop, valve throttling, level measurement.

PASCAL'S LAW: Pressure applied to confined fluid is transmitted undiminished throughout in all directions. F1/A1 = F2/A2.

HYDRAULIC PRESS APPLICATION: Input force on small piston creates pressure throughout system. Pressure acts on larger piston producing larger output force. Work In = Work Out: F1 x d1 = F2 x d2.

notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Valve Operational Issues and Prevention
notes.content:
THERMAL BINDING:

CAUSE: Valve body cools faster than disk during system cooldown. Differential thermal contraction binds seat against disk. Can make valve impossible to reopen until reheated.

PREVENTION:
• Periodically crack valve open and re-shut during cooldown
• Use proper closing torque (avoid over-torquing)
• Allow gradual cooling when possible

PRESSURE LOCKING:

CAUSE: Liquid trapped in valve bonnet pressurizes as it heats. Pressure buildup prevents valve opening. Common in high-temperature systems during startup.

PREVENTION:
• Provide relief or vent path on valve bonnet
• Equalize/reduce pressure buildup
• Design relief path to lower trapped pressure

OPERATING EXPERIENCE LESSONS:

1. Valve Handling: Never use wrench on valve handwheel for extra leverage. Can overstress actuator gearing/casing. Has caused catastrophic actuator failure.

2. Manual Operation Verification: Reaching hard stop on handwheel does NOT confirm full travel. Verify full designed number of turns. Position limit switch not reliable sole indicator.

3. Manual Override Precautions: Manually operating past designed open limit can slip position limit switch. Requires leak rate/stroke time retest after manual operation. Document any manual override operations.

KEY MAINTENANCE PRACTICES:
• Regular inspection for leakage
• Packing maintenance using backseat feature
• Periodic exercise to prevent stiction
• Monitor position indicators for consistency
• Track valve operation history

notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Valve Actuators and Position Verification
notes.content:
ACTUATOR FAILURE POSITIONS:
• Fail Open: opens on loss of power/control (safety for emergency drainage)
• Fail Closed: closes on loss of power (safety for isolation)
• Fail As Is: remains in current position (most Motor Operated Valves)
• Fail Safe: the position required to protect the system/equipment

MANUAL ACTUATORS:
• Handwheel or lever: direct mechanical operation
• Reach rods: extends control distance
• Gearing: provides mechanical advantage for higher torque

ELECTRIC ACTUATORS:
• Motor Operated Valves (MOVs): Fail As Is, gear reduction for torque, declutch lever for manual/motor switching
• Solenoid Operated Valves: Two-position (on/off only), spring placement determines fail position

PNEUMATIC ACTUATORS:
• Gas cylinder, piston, diaphragm, or vane designs
• Quarter-turn designs for butterfly/plug valves
• Spring action determines fail position on air loss

HYDRAULIC ACTUATORS:
• Piston-driven for high-force applications
• Spring action determines fail position on pressure loss

VALVE POSITION VERIFICATION METHODS:
• Light indication: Red = Open, Green = Closed, Both lit = Mid-position/Throttled
• Travel indication: Rising stem position, local mechanical pointer
• Actual verification: Visual stem observation, handwheel turn count, quarter-turn valve lever position

HUMAN ERROR PREVENTION:
• Verify all local/remote indications before and after manipulation
• Follow procedures and require independent verification
• Confirm valves and instrumentation are labeled correctly

notes.subject: TH01
notes.title: Systems of Measurement and Unit Conversion
notes.content:
TWO SYSTEMS OF MEASUREMENT:

AMERICAN ENGINEERING SYSTEM (ENGLISH ENGINEERS SYSTEM):
• Force: pounds force (lbf), Mass: pounds mass (lbm)
• Temperature: Fahrenheit (°F), Pressure: psi
• Conversion: Work in Btu = (Force in lbf x Distance in ft) / 778

INTERNATIONAL SYSTEM (SI/METRIC):
• Force: Newtons (N), Mass: kilograms (kg)
• Temperature: Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K)
• Pressure: Pascal (Pa), kilopascal (kPa), or bar
• Simpler conversion based on powers of 10

UNIT CONVERSION PROCESS:
1. Identify given units and desired units
2. Write conversion factor: given units in denominator, desired units in numerator
3. Multiply original value by conversion factor
4. Cancel units and solve

COMMON CONVERSION FACTORS:
• 1 lbm = 0.4536 kg, 1 psi = 6.895 kPa
• K = C + 273.15, R = F + 459.67

notes.subject: TH01
notes.title: Temperature Scales Comparison
notes.content:
RELATIVE SCALES (reference point based):

Fahrenheit (°F):
• Freezing point of water: 32°F
• Boiling point of water: 212°F
• Interval: 180 degrees between freeze/boil
• Used in American Engineering System

Celsius (°C):
• Freezing point of water: 0°C
• Boiling point of water: 100°C
• Interval: 100 degrees between freeze/boil
• Formula: °C = (°F - 32) / 1.8

ABSOLUTE SCALES (zero = no molecular motion):

Rankine (°R):
• Corresponds to Fahrenheit scale
• Absolute zero: 0°R = -459.67°F
• Formula: °R = °F + 459.67
• Maintains 1°F per 1°R interval

Kelvin (K):
• Corresponds to Celsius scale
• Absolute zero: 0 K = -273.15°C
• Formula: K = °C + 273.15
• Maintains 1°C per 1 K interval

WHY ABSOLUTE TEMPERATURE REQUIRED: All thermodynamic equations require absolute temperature. Relative scales produce wrong results in calculations.

notes.subject: TH01
notes.title: System Types and Properties
notes.content:
DEFINITION OF A SYSTEM: A quantity of matter or space separated from surroundings by a boundary. Everything outside is the surroundings.

THREE SYSTEM TYPES:

ISOLATED SYSTEM: No mass transfer, no energy transfer. Theoretical ideal; real systems always exchange something.

CLOSED SYSTEM: No mass transfer, energy transfer (heat/work) may occur. Most common for thermodynamic analysis. Used for fixed mass of gas in cylinder, sealed reactor vessel, closed piping sections.

OPEN SYSTEM (CONTROL VOLUME): Mass transfer across boundary (inlet/outlet flows), energy transfer may occur. Most realistic for practical equipment. Used for turbines, compressors, boilers, pumps, condensers, pipes with flow.

WORKING FLUID: The fluid within a system that stores or transfers energy. Must be able to absorb and reject heat. Examples: steam, refrigerant, gas, water.

SYSTEM PROPERTIES:

INTENSIVE PROPERTIES (independent of mass): Pressure, Temperature, Density, Specific volume, Velocity, Height, Enthalpy, Entropy

EXTENSIVE PROPERTIES (dependent on mass): Volume, Mass, Weight, Internal energy, Enthalpy, Entropy. Total = mass x specific property.

SPECIFIC PROPERTIES: Intensive properties derived from extensive ones. Calculated as: specific property = extensive property / mass.

notes.subject: TH02
notes.title: First Law of Thermodynamics
notes.content:
FIRST LAW OF THERMODYNAMICS:

STATEMENT: Energy is neither created nor destroyed, only converted between forms. Energy entering a system equals energy stored in or leaving it. E_in - E_out = Delta_E_system.

APPLICATION TO POWER SYSTEMS:
1. Heat Transfer: Heat input from energy source to working fluid
2. Fluid Conversion: Energy transfer to working fluid
3. Work Extraction: Expanding fluid performs mechanical work in turbine
4. Generator Conversion: Mechanical work converts to electrical energy
5. Heat Rejection: Low-pressure fluid exhausted to heat sink (condenser)
6. Pump Work: Fluid pressure increased for return to system

ENERGY BALANCE: Total energy in from heat source = Electrical power out + Heat rejected.
Efficiency = Electrical out / Total thermal in x 100%.

STORED vs. TRANSIENT ENERGY:

STORED ENERGY (energy a mass HAS): Potential Energy, Kinetic Energy, Internal Energy, Flow Energy

TRANSIENT ENERGY (energy IN TRANSIT): Heat (energy transfer due to temperature difference), Work (energy transfer due to force acting through distance)

SIGN CONVENTIONS:
• Heat added to system: Q positive, Heat removed: Q negative
• Work done BY system: W positive, Work done ON system: W negative

notes.subject: TH02
notes.title: Energy Components and Enthalpy
notes.content:
ENERGY FORMS IN THERMODYNAMIC SYSTEMS:

POTENTIAL ENERGY (PE): Due to position above reference point. Formula: PE = m x z x g / gc.

KINETIC ENERGY (KE): Due to motion/velocity. Formula: KE = m x v2 / (2 x gc).

INTERNAL ENERGY (U): Sum of all microscopic energy forms within substance. Specific internal energy u = U/m. Changes with temperature and pressure.

FLOW ENERGY (PV ENERGY): Energy a fluid has due to its pressure and volume. Specific flow energy = P x v. The work needed to push fluid into/out of system.

ENTHALPY (H): H = U + PV. Enthalpy = Internal Energy + Flow Work. Measure of a fluid's total energy content.

SPECIFIC ENTHALPY (h): h = u + Pv. Enthalpy per unit mass. Units: Btu/lbm. Used for all thermodynamic cycle calculations. Found in steam tables.

WHY ENTHALPY MATTERS:
• Directly represents energy available from working fluid
• Used to calculate turbine work: Wturbine = hin - hout
• Used to calculate pump work: Wpump = hout - hin
• Simplifies energy balance calculations for flowing systems
• Steam tables provide direct values

notes.subject: TH02
notes.title: Heat, Work, and Power
notes.content:
HEAT (Q):

DEFINITION: Transient energy transferred due to temperature difference. Flows from hotter to cooler. Spontaneous (no work required). NOT a property. Path-dependent.

WORK (W):

DEFINITION: Transient energy from force acting through distance. Formula: W = F x d. Units: Btu, ft-lbf, Joules. NOT a property (depends on process).

TWO TYPES:
1. Mechanical Work: Solid object moved by force. W = F x Delta_x.
2. Flow Work: Energy to push/pull fluid through pipes. P x V energy. Included in enthalpy.

TURBINE/PUMP WORK:
• Turbine: Input high-pressure steam, Output mechanical work + low-pressure steam. Work = hin - hout.
• Pump: Input low-pressure fluid + mechanical work, Output high-pressure fluid. Work = hout - hin.

POWER:

DEFINITION: Rate of doing work. Power = Work / Time. Units: Btu/s, Watts, horsepower. Conversion: 1 hp = 0.707 Btu/s = 746 Watts.

APPLICATIONS:
• Turbine power = specific work x mass flow rate
• Pump power = specific work x mass flow rate
• Generator power = turbine power - friction losses

notes.subject: TH03
notes.title: States, Phases, and Phase Diagrams
notes.content:
STATE OF A SUBSTANCE: Condition at specific moment, uniquely defined by two independent intensive properties. Once two properties known, all others can be determined via tables or equations.

PHASE: Describes molecular structure. Four phases: Solid (fixed shape/volume), Liquid (fixed volume, takes container shape), Vapor/Gas (no fixed shape/volume), Plasma (ionized gas).

PHASE CHANGES:
• Melting: Solid to Liquid (absorbed heat at constant pressure)
• Solidification: Liquid to Solid (rejected heat)
• Vaporization: Liquid to Gas (absorbed heat)
• Condensation: Gas to Liquid (rejected heat)
• Sublimation: Solid to Gas directly (skips liquid)

PHASE DIAGRAM (PRESSURE-TEMPERATURE):
• Fusion line: Solid-Liquid boundary
• Vaporization line: Liquid-Gas boundary
• Sublimation line: Solid-Gas boundary
• Triple Point: Where all three phases coexist in equilibrium
• Critical Point: Highest T and P where liquid and gas distinguish

PHASE DIAGRAM (PRESSURE-VOLUME): Vapor dome region where liquid and vapor coexist. Left of dome: subcooled liquid. Right of dome: superheated vapor.

notes.subject: TH03
notes.title: Five States of Water
notes.content:
FIVE STATES OF WATER:

1. SUBCOOLED LIQUID: Below saturation temperature. Resistant to vaporization. Nearly incompressible.
2. SATURATED LIQUID: At saturation temperature. On verge of boiling. All liquid molecules condensed.
3. WET VAPOR: Mixture of saturated liquid and vapor. Both phases coexist at same temperature.
4. SATURATED VAPOR: 100% vapor at saturation. Last drop of liquid just evaporated. Critical for turbine inlet quality.
5. SUPERHEATED VAPOR: Above saturation temperature. All liquid converted. Most flexible state.

HEAT TRANSFER DURING PHASE CHANGE:

SENSIBLE HEAT: Heat addition/removal that changes TEMPERATURE. No phase change. Observable temperature change.

LATENT HEAT: Heat addition/removal that causes PHASE CHANGE at constant temperature. All energy goes into changing molecular structure/bonds.

LATENT HEAT OF VAPORIZATION (LHV/Hfg): Energy needed to convert saturated liquid to saturated vapor. Same energy released when vapor condenses. Decreases as pressure increases.

LATENT HEAT OF CONDENSATION: Opposite of vaporization. Energy released when steam condenses to liquid.

STEAM QUALITY (X) AND MOISTURE CONTENT (M):
• Quality: Fraction of vapor in wet mixture. Range 0 (liquid) to 1.0 (vapor).
• Moisture content: Fraction of liquid. M = 1 - X.
• Void fraction: Volumetric fraction of vapor (not mass fraction).

IMPORTANCE OF STEAM QUALITY: High quality required at turbine inlet (>95-99%). Low quality causes blade erosion and corrosion.

notes.subject: TH03
notes.title: Ideal Gas Laws and Steam Tables
notes.content:
IDEAL GAS BEHAVIOR:

CHARLES' LAW (constant pressure): Gas volume directly proportional to absolute temperature. V/T = constant. V1/T1 = V2/T2.

BOYLE'S LAW (constant temperature): Gas volume inversely proportional to absolute pressure. P x V = constant. P1V1 = P2V2.

COMBINED/IDEAL GAS LAW: Combines Charles and Boyle. Equation: PV/T = nR or PV = nRT. Specific form: Pv = RT for one lbm of gas.

APPLICATIONS: Air and gas systems, low pressures, high temperatures. Does NOT work for steam near saturation.

WHY IDEAL GAS LAW FAILS FOR STEAM: Steam behavior MORE COMPLEX. Intermolecular forces significant. Proximity to saturation violates ideal assumptions. PV/T NOT constant.

STEAM TABLES: Experimentally-measured properties of water and steam. Account for complex non-ideal behavior. Tabulated for common pressures/temperatures.

TABLE TYPES:
• Saturated steam tables: Indexed by saturation temperature or pressure. Properties at saturation point.
• Superheated steam tables: Indexed by pressure and temperature. For steam ABOVE saturation.
• Compressed liquid tables: Properties below saturation temperature.

DEGREES OF SUBCOOLING: Difference between saturation and actual liquid temperature. Formula: Degrees subcooling = Tsat - Tactual.

DEGREES OF SUPERHEAT: Difference between actual and saturation temperature. Formula: Degrees superheat = Tactual - Tsat.

notes.subject: TH04
notes.title: Thermodynamic Processes
notes.content:
PROCESS DEFINITIONS:

ADIABATIC PROCESS: No loss or gain of heat (Q = 0). Boundary perfectly insulated. Common for turbine/pump (very fast). Analysis: W = Delta_U.

ISOBARIC PROCESS: Constant pressure (P = constant). Boundary moves freely. Heat addition/removal at constant pressure. Common: boiler steam generation, condenser condensation. Analysis: Q = Delta_H.

ISENTROPIC PROCESS: No change in entropy (S = constant). Reversible adiabatic. Theoretical ideal (never achieved in reality). Benchmark for efficiency calculations.

ISENTHALPIC PROCESS: No change in enthalpy (H = constant). Also called throttling. Through partial valve opening, orifice, restriction. Effect: P decreases, T may decrease, entropy increases.

ISOTHERMAL PROCESS: Constant temperature (T = constant). System remains in thermal equilibrium. For ideal gas: PV = constant. Analysis: Temperature constant.

OTHER PROCESS TYPES:
• Isochoric: constant volume (rigid container)
• Polytropic: generalized process
• Reversible: frictionless, no net change
• Irreversible: real process with friction/losses

notes.subject: TH04
notes.title: Steady Flow and Second Law
notes.content:
STEADY FLOW SYSTEM CONDITIONS:

THREE REQUIREMENTS:
1. CONSTANT MASS FLOW RATE: Mass in per time = mass out per time. Continuity: m_in = m_out.
2. CONSTANT WORKING-FLUID PROPERTIES: Properties (P,T,h,s,v) constant over time at each point. May vary spatially.
3. CONSTANT RATES OF HEAT/WORK: Q-dot constant, W-dot constant. Not fluctuating.

IMPORTANCE: Most practical equipment operates under steady-flow. Simplifies analysis. Energy balance: Q-dot + m-dot(hin) = m-dot(hout) + W-dot.

MECHANICAL EQUIVALENT OF HEAT: 1 Btu = 778 ft-lbf or 1055 Joules. Converts between mechanical and thermal energy.

SECOND LAW OF THERMODYNAMICS:

STATEMENT: No engine can convert all heat to work. Heat rejection always required. Even perfect engines cannot be 100% efficient. eta_max = 1 - (Tcold / Thot).

REVERSIBLE vs IRREVERSIBLE:
• Reversible (ideal): Theoretical, no friction, frictionless, returns to original state
• Irreversible (real): All actual processes. Friction present. Cannot return without external work.

ENTROPY CHANGE: Delta_S = Q / T. Units: Btu/lbm-R. More heat at low temperature = larger entropy change.

CARNOT EFFICIENCY: eta_Carnot = (Thot - Tcold) / Thot = 1 - (Tcold / Thot). Depends ONLY on temperatures.

notes.subject: TH04
notes.title: Heat Exchangers and Nozzles
notes.content:
HEAT EXCHANGER ENERGY BALANCE: q = hout - hin. Heat transfer = enthalpy change. No work done (W = 0). All heat change as enthalpy change.

STEAM GENERATOR (BOILER): Raises feedwater enthalpy to produce steam. Isobaric heat addition. Inlet: subcooled liquid. Outlet: saturated/near-saturated steam.

CONDENSER: Removes latent heat of condensation. Isobaric heat rejection. Inlet: low-pressure wet/saturated steam. Outlet: subcooled liquid condensate. Critical: condensing collapses steam volume, maintains vacuum, increases turbine pressure drop, improves efficiency.

CONDENSATE SUBCOOLING: After condensation, cooling continues below saturation. Few degrees required to prevent pump cavitation. Excessive subcooling wastes heat and increases corrosion. Optimal: 10-20F.

NOZZLES:

DEFINITION: Shaped passages converting pressure/enthalpy to velocity/kinetic energy. No work (W=0). No heat transfer (adiabatic).

CONVERGENT NOZZLE: Area decreases. Velocity increases, pressure decreases, temperature decreases. Used for accelerating steam.

DIVERGENT NOZZLE: Area increases. Velocity decreases, pressure increases, temperature increases. Used for slowing flow, recovering pressure.

CONTINUITY EQUATION: A1V1 = A2V2. Shrinking area increases velocity. Maintains constant mass flow rate.

NOZZLE APPLICATIONS: Flow measurement, flow restriction, pressure recovery, turbine nozzles, steam jet air ejectors.

notes.subject: TH04
notes.title: Turbines, Pumps, and Throttles
notes.content:
TURBINE - WORK EXTRACTION: WT = hin - hout. Enthalpy drop across turbine. High-pressure steam in, low-pressure out. Expansion converted to rotating mechanical energy.

TWO-STEP CONVERSION:
1. NOZZLE STAGE (Fixed Blades): Converts pressure/enthalpy to kinetic energy. High-pressure low-velocity steam enters. Convergent nozzle accelerates. Low-pressure high-velocity jet exits.
2. BLADE STAGE (Moving Blades): Jet impacts rotating blades. Blade motion redirects flow. Kinetic energy to mechanical rotation.

TURBINE EFFICIENCY: eta_turbine = Wreal / Wideal. Ratio of actual to ideal work. Typical: 80-90%. eta = (h1-h2)/(h1-h2s).

PUMP - WORK INPUT: WP = hout - hin. Enthalpy rise. Motor does work ON pump. Increases fluid pressure.

PUMP EFFICIENCY: eta_pump = Wideal / Wreal (opposite of turbine!). Ratio ideal to actual work. Typical: 75-85%. eta = (h2s-h1)/(h2-h1).

CYCLE WORK ACCOUNTING: Wnet = Wturbine - Wpump. Pump work 1-5% of turbine work.

THROTTLE (THROTTLING): Constant-enthalpy through restriction. P decreases, h constant, entropy increases, T may decrease. Examples: pressure reducing valve, orifice, pipe restriction.

FINDING DOWNSTREAM STATE: Use h2 = h1. At P2, find saturation enthalpy. If h1 < hf: compressed liquid. If hf < h1 < hg: quality X = (h1-hf)/hfg. If h1 > hg: superheated.

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Thermodynamic Cycles - Fundamentals
notes.content:
FIVE ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS OF A THERMODYNAMIC CYCLE:

1. A Working Fluid: Substance operating through cycle (typically steam or gas). Must absorb and reject heat. Properties change through processes.

2. An Engine: Device converting heat energy to mechanical work. Examples: turbines, pistons, compressors. Efficiency determines heat conversion.

3. A Heat Source: Supplies thermal energy to working fluid. Examples: boiler, furnace, solar radiation. Temperature affects cycle efficiency.

4. A Heat Sink: Absorbs waste heat rejected by cycle. Examples: condenser, cooling tower, atmosphere. Lower temperature improves efficiency.

5. A Device to Move Working Fluid: Pump or compressor circulates working fluid. Requires work input. Necessary for continuous cycle operation.

CYCLE EFFICIENCY: Cycle efficiency = net work out / heat added = (QA - QR) / QA. The Second Law requires some heat always be rejected.

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: The Carnot Cycle
notes.content:
THE CARNOT CYCLE: Theoretical maximum efficiency standard.

FOUR PROCESSES:

1. ISOTHERMAL EXPANSION (1 to 2): Temperature constant. Heat absorbed from heat source. Pressure decreases, volume increases. Work output by system.

2. ADIABATIC EXPANSION (2 to 3): No heat transfer. Temperature and pressure decrease. Working fluid expands against turbine. Maximum work extracted.

3. ISOTHERMAL COMPRESSION (3 to 4): Temperature constant. Heat rejected to heat sink. Volume decreases, pressure increases. Work input to system.

4. ADIABATIC COMPRESSION (4 to 1): No heat transfer. Temperature and pressure increase. Returns to initial state. Work input to system.

CARNOT EFFICIENCY FORMULA: eta = 1 - (Tcold / Thot). Where Tcold and Thot are absolute temperatures (Kelvin). Depends ONLY on temperature ratio. Independent of working fluid type.

KEY PRINCIPLE: Highest possible efficiency for any engine operating between given heat source and heat sink. Real cycles always fall short due to friction and non-reversible processes. Carnot sets theoretical maximum.

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Rankine Cycle - Realistic Steam Cycle
notes.content:
THE RANKINE CYCLE:

OVERVIEW: More realistic steam cycle used in actual power plants. Operates with four main processes using steam as working fluid.

FOUR PROCESSES:

1. PUMP COMPRESSION (1→2): Adiabatic compression. Increases liquid pressure. Pump requires work input (negative work output).

2. HEAT ADDITION (2→3): Constant-pressure (isobaric) process. Boiler/steam generator raises feedwater enthalpy. Converts subcooled liquid to saturated or superheated steam.

3. TURBINE EXPANSION (3→4): Adiabatic expansion. Decreases pressure and enthalpy. Produces useful work output. Turbine extracts energy from steam.

4. HEAT REJECTION (4→1): Constant-pressure (isobaric) process. Condenser removes latent heat of condensation. Converts exhausted steam to liquid for pump return.

NET WORK: Wnet = Wturbine - Wpump. Pump work typically 1-5% of turbine work.

VS. CARNOT CYCLE:
• More realistic: uses isobars instead of isotherms
• All pressure changes occur at pump & turbine points
• Easier to analyze using steam tables
• Lower efficiency than Carnot but achievable in practice

EFFICIENCY:
• Efficiency computed from enthalpies using steam tables
• Real turbines and pumps add entropy (friction losses)
• Efficiency lower than ideal due to irreversibilities

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Improving Cycle Efficiency
notes.content:
HOW TO INCREASE CYCLE EFFICIENCY:

TWO PRIMARY METHODS BASED ON CARNOT:

1. INCREASE HEAT SOURCE TEMPERATURE (Thot):
• More energy input from higher temperature source
• Larger temperature difference with heat sink
• Efficiency gain proportional to temperature increase
• Example: Superheating steam in boiler
• Practical limit: Material strength at high temperatures
• Method: Increase boiler temperature/pressure

2. DECREASE HEAT SINK TEMPERATURE (Tcold):
• Larger temperature difference with heat source
• More work extracted per unit of heat input
• Example: Improving condenser cooling (vacuum)
• Practical limit: Ambient temperature (cannot go below)
• Deep vacuum in condenser maximizes this effect
• Method: Improve condenser cooling efficiency

ADDITIONAL RANKINE CYCLE IMPROVEMENTS:

1. SUPERHEATING THE STEAM:
• Increases efficiency (more net work than added heat rejected)
• Raises average temperature of heat addition
• Reduces moisture content in low-pressure turbine

2. FEEDWATER HEATING:
• Extraction steam preheats feedwater
• Increases efficiency: less heat needed from heat source
• Typical improvement: 1-3% per extraction point
• Modern plants often have 3-8 feedwater heaters

3. MOISTURE SEPARATOR REHEATER (MSR):
• Protects turbine blades from erosion
• Minor effect on cycle efficiency
• Real purpose: drying/superheating steam to LP turbine

4. HIGHER STEAM PRESSURE:
• Increases efficiency: lower entropy in and out
• More turbine work available
• Limited by material properties

5. HIGHER STEAM QUALITY:
• More enthalpy available for net work
• Minimum quality >95-99% to prevent blade damage
• Condenser vacuum improvements help maintain quality

OPERATIONAL FOCUS:
• Efficiency best at full load
• Minimize auxiliaries and leaks
• Fix steam leaks and air leaks
• Regular maintenance and cleaning
• Proper operator technique and experience

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Condenser Vacuum and Heat Rate
notes.content:
CONDENSER VACUUM: CRITICAL FACTOR IN EFFICIENCY

WHAT IS CONDENSER VACUUM:
• Pressure inside condenser lower than atmospheric
• Expressed as absolute pressure (psia) or inches of mercury
• Example: 1 psia absolute = about 29 inches mercury vacuum
• Lower absolute pressure = higher vacuum = better efficiency

WHY LOWER PRESSURE INCREASES EFFICIENCY:

1. INCREASED EXPANSION RATIO:
• Pressure drop across turbine: from boiler to condenser
• Lower condenser pressure = larger expansion ratio
• More work extracted per unit of steam
• Turbine does more work with same steam flow

2. MORE WORK OUTPUT:
• At atmospheric condenser: less work extracted
• At vacuum condenser: more work extracted from same steam
• Extra work directly increases efficiency

3. FORMULA RELATIONSHIP:
• Carnot efficiency: eta = 1 - (Tcold/Thot)
• Lower pressure = lower temperature of condenser
• Larger T_hot/T_cold ratio = higher efficiency

IMPROVING CONDENSER VACUUM:

1. COOLING WATER FLOW:
• More/cooler cooling water improves heat rejection
• Lowers steam condensation temperature
• Maintains lower absolute pressure

2. AIR REMOVAL:
• Vacuum pump removes air from condenser
• Air reduces effective pressure
• Regular maintenance keeps pump effective

3. CONDENSER TUBE CLEANING:
• Fouling reduces heat transfer
• Clean tubes allow better cooling
• Regular cleaning improves vacuum

4. TEMPERATURE MANAGEMENT:
• Cooling water temperature affects vacuum
• Seasonal variations impact performance
• Cold water = better vacuum = higher efficiency

PRACTICAL IMPACT:
• Each inch mercury improvement in vacuum: increases efficiency ~0.5-1%
• Increases power output
• Major focus area for optimization
• Cost-effective improvement strategy
• Monitored continuously

HEAT RATE: MEASURE OF PLANT PERFORMANCE

DEFINITION: Heat Rate = Energy Input (BTU) / Electrical Output (kW-hr). Units: BTU/kW-hr (typical range 7,000-10,000).

EXAMPLE: Plant with heat rate 8,000 BTU/kW-hr: To produce 1 kW-hr electricity requires 8,000 BTU fuel input. Waste: 4,588 BTU (1 kW-hr = 3,412 BTU).

RELATIONSHIP TO EFFICIENCY:
• Inverse relationship: Lower heat rate = Higher efficiency
• Efficiency (%) = 3,412 / Heat Rate x 100
• Example: 3,412 / 8,000 x 100 = 42.65% efficiency

WHY IT MATTERS:
• Economic: 1% improvement = significant fuel cost savings
• Environmental: Lower heat rate = less fuel burned, reduced emissions
• Operational: Real-time measure of plant performance, indicates maintenance needs

IMPROVING HEAT RATE:
• Condenser vacuum improvements
• Feedwater heating optimization
• Moisture separator reheater tuning
• Reducing auxiliary power consumption
• Regular maintenance and cleaning
• Operator skill and experience

INDUSTRY BENCHMARKS:
• Best-in-class plants: ~8,200 BTU/kW-hr
• Average plants: ~9,000-9,500 BTU/kW-hr
• Older plants: >10,000 BTU/kW-hr

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Mass Flow Rate
notes.content: The mass of fluid passing a reference point per unit time. Formula: ṁ = ρ × V̇ = ρ × v × A. Units: lbm/hr. Where ρ = density (lbm/ft³), v = average velocity (ft/hr), A = cross-sectional area (ft²)

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Volumetric Flow Rate
notes.content: The volume of fluid passing a reference point per unit time. Formula: Q = V̇ = v × A. Common units: gallons per minute (gpm) or cubic feet per minute (cfm). Related to mass flow rate by: ṁ = ρ × Q

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Steady Flow (Continuity of Flow)
notes.content: Flow condition where the mass flow rate into and out of a system or component are equal. Mathematically: ṁ₁ = ṁ₂. This ensures the same quantity of fluid flows past any reference points along a pipe in a given time.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Viscosity
notes.content: A measure of a fluid's ability to resist flow. Water at 68°F = 1.0 centipoise (reference standard). For liquids: viscosity DECREASES with increasing temperature. For gases: viscosity INCREASES with increasing temperature. Higher viscosity requires more energy for flow.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Specific Gravity
notes.content: The ratio of a fluid's density compared to water at 60°F. Water has SG = 1.0. Fluids with SG < 1 float in water. Fluids with SG > 1 sink in water. As SG increases, more energy required for flow.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Density
notes.content: The mass per unit volume of a substance. For water: approximately 62.4 lbm/ft³ at standard conditions (atmospheric pressure, ~53°F). Density changes with both temperature and pressure. Specific volume (v) is the reciprocal of density.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Laminar Flow
notes.content: Flow regime where fluid layers move smoothly over adjacent layers with minimal mixing. Occurs when Reynolds number NRe < 2,000. Velocity profile is PARABOLIC - zero at walls, maximum at center. Average velocity = half the centerline velocity.
notes.imageLabel: Laminar Flow Velocity Profile - Parabolic Distribution
notes.imageLabel2: Laminar Flow Alternative View - Parallel Plates

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Turbulent Flow
notes.content: Flow regime characterized by cross-currents that disturb and mix fluid layers. Occurs when Reynolds number NRe > 3,500. Velocity profile is FLATTENED at centerline. Average velocity is close to centerline velocity. Requires more energy than laminar flow.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Transitional Flow
notes.content: Flow regime between laminar and turbulent. Occurs when Reynolds number is between 2,000 and 3,500. Flow is unstable and alternates between laminar and turbulent characteristics.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Reynolds Number
notes.content: Dimensionless number determining flow type. Formula: NRe = (ρ × v × d) / μ. Where ρ = density, v = velocity, d = pipe diameter, μ = dynamic viscosity. Determines whether flow is laminar, transitional, or turbulent.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Single-Phase Flow
notes.content: Flow where the fluid is completely in either liquid state OR gaseous state, not a mixture. Most fluid systems are designed for single-phase flow. Easier to analyze and predict than two-phase flow.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Two-Phase Flow
notes.content: Flow condition where fluid is a mixture of liquid AND vapor phases (e.g., water and steam mixture). Creates much greater resistance to flow than single-phase. Head loss can increase by factor of 100+ as quality increases from 0% to 100%.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Elevation Head
notes.content: The potential energy component of Bernoulli's equation. Represented by variable 'z' = height above reference point (in feet). Energy of fluid due to its elevation in gravitational field. Same regardless of path to reach that elevation.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Velocity Head
notes.content: The kinetic energy component of Bernoulli's equation. Formula: v²/(2gc). The height to which flowing fluid would rise in a column if ALL kinetic energy converted to potential energy. Units: feet of head.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Pressure Head
notes.content: The pressure-volume energy component of Bernoulli's equation. Represented by Pv. Height (in feet) of a fluid column whose weight equals the pressure of the fluid. Accounts for static pressure energy.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Total Head
notes.content: Sum of elevation head (z), velocity head (v²/2gc), and pressure head (Pv). Bernoulli's equation states that total head is constant in ideal flow (no friction, no heat transfer, no work done on/by fluid).

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Head Loss
notes.content: The conversion of fluid pressure and velocity to heat energy through friction. Represents energy lost from fluid flow perspective, but increases internal energy (temperature). Expressed in feet of head. Denoted as Hf in modified Bernoulli's equation.
notes.imageLabel: System Characteristic Curves - Head Loss vs Volumetric Flow Rate

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Friction Factor
notes.content: Dimensionless proportionality constant in Darcy's equation accounting for pipe surface roughness. Depends on Reynolds number (laminar vs turbulent) and degree of pipe roughness. Higher friction factor → higher head loss.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Cavitation
notes.content: Phenomenon where fluid vapor bubbles form when local pressure drops below saturation pressure. Occurs at pump suction with inadequate NPSH. Causes serious damage to pump impellers. Common cause: insufficient system pressure at pump inlet or excessive heat.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Sonic Choking
notes.content: Phenomenon in compressible flow (steam) where velocity reaches Mach 1 in pipe restriction. Pressure wave forms that opposes applied pressure, limiting mass flow even if downstream pressure decreases. For gases: occurs when Pout/Pin ≈ 0.5

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Water Hammer
notes.content: Pressure fluctuations/surges in piping system when flow is suddenly changed (e.g., rapid valve closure or pump start/stop). Creates sonic wave that travels through system. Can cause pressure spikes up to 20× normal system pressure. Results in pipe damage, component displacement, and potential personnel injury.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Definition: Pump Head
notes.content: Head (energy) added to fluid by pump. Represented as Hp in modified Bernoulli's equation. Pump does work on fluid (Wp) to overcome friction losses and increase system pressure. Work done by pump: Wp = ṁ × Hp × gc/g

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Mass Flow Rate
notes.content: ṁ = ρ × V̇ = ρ × v × A = (v × A) / v. Where: ṁ = mass flow rate (lbm/hr), ρ = density (lbm/ft³), V̇ = volumetric flow rate (ft³/hr), v = average velocity (ft/hr), A = cross-sectional area (ft²), v = specific volume (ft³/lbm)

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Volumetric Flow Rate
notes.content: Q = V̇ = v × A. Where: Q = volumetric flow rate (ft³/hr), v = average velocity (ft/hr), A = cross-sectional area (ft²). Related to mass flow by: ṁ = ρ × Q or ṁ = Q / v

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Continuity (Incompressible Fluid)
notes.content: v₁ × A₁ = v₂ × A₂. For incompressible fluids flowing through pipe of varying diameter: when diameter increases, velocity decreases proportionally. When diameter decreases, velocity increases proportionally. Mass flow rate ṁ remains constant.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Reynolds Number
notes.content: NRe = (ρ × v × d) / μ = (v × d) / ν. Where: NRe = Reynolds number (dimensionless), ρ = density (lbm/ft³), v = average velocity (ft/sec), d = pipe diameter (ft), μ = dynamic viscosity (lbm/ft-sec), ν = kinematic viscosity (ft²/sec)

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Bernoulli's (Ideal Flow)
notes.content: z₁ + v₁²/(2gc) + Pv₁ = z₂ + v₂²/(2gc) + Pv₂. Total head is constant (no friction, no work). Where: z = elevation head (ft), v²/(2gc) = velocity head (ft), Pv = pressure head (ft)

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Bernoulli's (Real Flow - Modified)
notes.content: z₁ + v₁²/(2gc) + Pv₁ + Hp = z₂ + v₂²/(2gc) + Pv₂ + Hf. Includes pump head (Hp) added and friction head (Hf) lost. Accounts for real-world friction and pump work. gc = gravitational constant (32.2 ft-lbm/lbf-sec²)

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Flow-Pressure Relationship (Leak)
notes.content: F ∝ √ΔP. For subcooled liquid leaks: flow is proportional to square root of pressure drop. If pressure doubles (2×), flow increases by √2 ≈ 1.41×. At very high velocities or with compressible fluids: F ∝ ΔP

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Darcy's Equation (Head Loss)
notes.content: Hf = f × (L/d) × (v²/(2gc)). Where: Hf = head loss (ft), f = friction factor, L = pipe length (ft), d = pipe diameter (ft), v = average velocity (ft/sec), gc = 32.2 ft-lbm/lbf-sec². Head loss ↑ with ↑L, ↑f, ↑v²; ↓ with ↑d

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Head Loss (Level Pipe, Constant Area)
notes.content: Hf = ΔP / (ρ/gc). For level pipe with constant diameter: pressure drop equals head loss. ΔP = Hf × ρ / gc × g. For incompressible fluids (liquids): simplified to ΔP = Hf × ρ / gc

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Joukowsky Equation (Water Hammer)
notes.content: ΔP = ρ × c × Δv. Where: ΔP = pressure change from water hammer (psi), ρ = fluid density, c = speed of sound in fluid (ft/sec), Δv = change in velocity. Peak pressure depends on velocity change and fluid density, NOT pipe length.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Equation: Water Hammer Pressure Pulse Duration
notes.content: Tpp = 2L / c. Where: Tpp = duration of pressure pulse (sec), L = straight pipe length (ft), c = speed of sound in fluid (ft/sec). Longer pipes = longer duration of pressure spike.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Concept: Impact of Viscosity on System Operation
notes.content: Higher viscosity = more energy required for flow. For liquids: viscosity decreases with temperature (easier flow when hot). For gases: viscosity increases with temperature. Important for startup: cold oil in motor bearings can cause high viscosity and bearing damage if started too cold. Minimum bearing temperatures required before pump start.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Concept: Impact of Temperature on System Properties
notes.content: For liquids with increasing temperature: viscosity DECREASES, specific gravity DECREASES, density DECREASES. Net effect: less energy required for flow. BUT: as heat added to closed system, pressure INCREASES (confined volume). Risk: heat exchanger rupture if isolated before cooling.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Concept: Laminar vs Turbulent Flow - Head Loss
notes.content: Laminar flow (NRe < 2,000): smooth, layered, less turbulence, lower head loss for given velocity. Turbulent flow (NRe > 3,500): chaotic, cross-currents, higher head loss. Velocity profile determines friction. Transitional (2,000-3,500): unstable, alternates between types.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Concept: How Operators Control Head Loss
notes.content: Throttle valve position: closing valve increases L/d ratio → head loss increases. Opening valve decreases L/d → head loss decreases. Pump speed variation: since Hf ∝ v², speed change causes exponential Hf change. Series vs parallel piping: series → Hf increases, parallel → Hf decreases
notes.imageLabel: Fluid Flow in Closed System - Pump, Valves, and Head Loss Equations

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Concept: Velocity Effect on Head Loss
notes.content: Head loss is EXPONENTIAL with velocity: Hf ∝ v². If velocity doubles (2×), head loss increases by (2)² = 4× (quadruples). If velocity triples (3×), head loss increases by (3)² = 9×. Small velocity increases cause large head loss increases.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Concept: Two-Phase Flow in Steam Generator
notes.content: In steam generator tube bundle region: water/steam mixture flows simultaneously. Two-phase friction much greater than single-phase. Can cause sudden pressure loss changes → water hammer. Formation/collapse of vapor bubbles cause pressure fluctuations. Flow oscillations difficult to control.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Safety: Water Hammer Prevention - System Preparation
notes.content: 1. Ensure systems are water-solid (completely filled) before starting pumps. 2. Open high-point casing vents to remove gases. 3. Keep fill systems for low-pressure cooling systems. 4. Remove air from system before startup. 5. Keep vents open until solid stream of fluid comes out.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Safety: Water Hammer Prevention - Operational Procedures
notes.content: 1. Initiate flow SLOWLY when starting pumps. 2. Close valves SLOWLY when stopping flow (NOT rapidly). 3. Prevent hot steam flow into cool water systems. 4. Prevent cold water introduction into hot steam systems. 5. Ensure steam traps functional to remove condensate. 6. Install drains at pipe low points to prevent water pockets.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Safety: Water Hammer Prevention - System Design
notes.content: 1. Avoid long horizontal pipes connecting steam-filled tanks with water sources (vulnerable to backflow). 2. Systems with large elevation differences need keep-fill capability. 3. Install drains on low points to prevent water accumulation and steam pocket collapse. 4. Valve materials and design reduce slam effects. 5. Maintenance programs critical for valve performance.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Safety: NPSH and Cavitation in Closed Systems
notes.content: As closed system heats up: temperature approaches saturation point. Lowest pressure is at pump suction. When saturation reached at suction: water flashes to steam = cavitation. Cavitation damages pump impeller. Risk increases with higher system temperature. Solution: maintain adequate system pressure or limit operating temperature margins.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Safety: System Draining and Startup
notes.content: Before draining: stop pumps and tag power sources. DO NOT start any pump in a drained system. Pump damage risk from: cavitation (no cooling liquid), gas binding (air in case), metal-to-metal friction. Always ensure system properly filled and vented before startup. High-point vents must be open until solid fluid stream appears.

notes.subject: Fluid Flow (TH06B)
notes.title: Key Operator Focus: Monitoring and Understanding Flow
notes.content: Know where pressure and flow indications are located. Verify parameters change as expected based on: operating curves, fluid flow fundamentals, system characteristics. Question unexpected indications or out-of-ordinary conditions. Don't allow system changes that erode margins to conservative operation. Understand how component operation affects system before acting. Predict effects, then verify actual results.
